docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
• ECCCH — The entire Cultural Heritage Cloud, or ECCCH for short.
    • Data — This includes the digital heritage assets (digital representations of cultural heritage, such as images, audios and 3D models) that serve as the foundational building blocks for heritage representations within the cloud, as well as the metadata and semantic knowledge accessible through the Cultural Heritage Cloud.
      • Sister Project Data — Data produced by sister projects (e.g., AUTOMATA, TEXTaiLES, and HERITALISE), which are mandated to integrate their outcomes into the ECCCH [D3.1].
      • Cascading Grants Data — Data contributed by external consortia, such as Cultural Heritage Institutions (CHIs), funded through the ECHOES grant program [D3.1].
      • RI Data — Content provided by RIs such as DARIAH, CLARIN, and E-RIHS [D3.1].
      • National and Regional Initiative Data — Data from large-scale national projects (e.g., ESPADON in France or H2IOSC in Italy) or regional initiatives [D3.1].
      • Individual and Institutional Data — Data provided by individual or institutional researchers, cultural heritage professionals, and other stakeholders [D3.1].
    • Semantic Resources — This encompasses the conceptual and semantic resources that support the ECCCH ecosystem, including the ECHOES Data Model (Heritage Digital Twin Ontology, HDTO), controlled vocabularies, taxonomies, and related metadata schemas used to describe, interlink, and interpret cultural heritage assets.
      • ECHOES Semantic Resources — ECHOES will provide the Heritage Digital Twin Ontology (HDTO), the core data model for the cloud.
      • Sister Projects and Cascading Grants Resources — These providers contribute specialized ontologies, domain-specific extensions of standards like CIDOC CRM and localized data models tailored to specific research use cases such as textiles or archaeology.
      • RI and Umbrella Organizations Resources — Bodies such as DARIAH, CLARIN, and E-RIHS, along with networks like ICOMOS and NEMO, provide curated controlled vocabularies, and domain-specific standards [D5.3].
      • External Adopters and Individual CH Institutions — These providers contribute metadata mappings and institutional vocabularies to bridge local repositories with the ECCCH Knowledge Base.
    • Applications and Tools — The applications and other software tools provided by the Cultural Heritage Cloud to support tasks such as data management, visualization, analysis, and user interaction across the ECCCH ecosystem.
      • ECHOES Vertical Applications — The three initial domain-specific, vertical applications (VAs) developed by ECHOES: the Online Conservation Restoration Annotator (OCRA), the Virtual Transcription Laboratory (VTL), and the Collection Ingestion Tool (CIT).
      • Sister Projects and Cascading Grant Applications — This group consists of specialized tools developed by sister projects as well as applications developed by institutions through the Cascading Grants Programme.
      • Commercial Companies and Other Adopters — This category encompasses tools provided by the broader ecosystem, including private companies.
    • Infrastructure and Digital Services — The infrastructure that will provide access to CH data, metadata and digital services in a secure, federated environment. Sample services include user access management, data storage, data sharing, computing resources, search capabilities, workflow management, and Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) services.
      • ECHOES Infrastructure and Services — ECHOES is developing the core infrastructure and cloud components. These include the Single Entry Point (SEP), and services such as the Authentication and Authorisation Infrastructure (AAI) and the core Knowledge Base.
      • Sister Projects and Cascading Grant Services — These providers contribute specialized digital services and domain-specific tools.
      • RIs and National/International Services — These providers can offer federated nodes, distributed storage, and advanced computing resources. This category includes established European Research Infrastructures like DARIAH, CLARIN, and E-RIHS, national initiatives (e.g., ESPADON, H2IOSC), and large-scale providers of High-Performance Computing (HPC) and AI Factories (e.g., EuroHPC).
    • Community Services — Community-facing services such as workshops, seminars, and other training, engagement and networking programs to support ECCCH stakeholders.
      • ECHOES Services — Services provided by ECHOES team to ensure project-wide awareness and technical baseline readiness. Examples include training modules and consortium-led webinars [D5.3].
      • Sister Projects and Cascading Grant Services — Services provided by projects funded under ECCCH calls (e.g., AUTOMATA, TEXTaiLES) or through Cascading Grants. These include domain-specific mentorship and training for specific applications [D5.3].
      • RIs, Umbrella and Network Services — Services provided by established European bodies and professional umbrella organizations.

• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_RI_DATA
  • Level: 2
  • Description: Content provided by RIs such as DARIAH, CLARIN, and E-RIHS [D3.1].

docs.title: National and Regional Initiative Data
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_NATIONAL_AND_REGIONAL
  • Level: 2
  • Description: Data from large-scale national projects (e.g., ESPADON in France or H2IOSC in Italy) or regional initiatives [D3.1].

docs.title: Individual and Institutional Data
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_INDIVIDUAL_AND_INSTITUTIONAL
  • Level: 2
  • Description: Data provided by individual or institutional researchers, cultural heritage professionals, and other stakeholders [D3.1].

docs.title: Semantic Resources
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_SEMANTIC_RESOURCES
  • Level: 1
  • Description: This encompasses the conceptual and semantic resources that support the ECCCH ecosystem, including the ECHOES Data Model (Heritage Digital Twin Ontology, HDTO), controlled vocabularies, taxonomies, and related metadata schemas used to describe, interlink, and interpret cultural heritage assets.

docs.title: ECHOES Semantic Resources
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_ECHOES_SEMANTIC_RESOURCES
  • Level: 2
  • Description: ECHOES will provide the Heritage Digital Twin Ontology (HDTO), the core data model for the cloud.

docs.title: Sister Projects and Cascading Grants Resources
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_SISTER_PROJECTS_AND
  • Level: 2
  • Description: These providers contribute specialized ontologies, domain-specific extensions of standards like CIDOC CRM and localized data models tailored to specific research use cases such as textiles or archaeology.

docs.title: RI and Umbrella Organizations Resources
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_RI_AND_UMBRELLA
  • Level: 2
  • Description: Bodies such as DARIAH, CLARIN, and E-RIHS, along with networks like ICOMOS and NEMO, provide curated controlled vocabularies, and domain-specific standards [D5.3].

docs.title: External Adopters and Individual CH Institutions
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_EXTERNAL_ADOPTERS_AND
  • Level: 2
  • Description: These providers contribute metadata mappings and institutional vocabularies to bridge local repositories with the ECCCH Knowledge Base.

docs.title: Applications and Tools
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_APPLICATIONS_AND_TOOLS
  • Level: 1
  • Description: The applications and other software tools provided by the Cultural Heritage Cloud to support tasks such as data management, visualization, analysis, and user interaction across the ECCCH ecosystem.

docs.title: ECHOES Vertical Applications
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_ECHOES_VERTICAL_APPLICATIONS
  • Level: 2
  • Description: The three initial domain-specific, vertical applications (VAs) developed by ECHOES: the Online Conservation Restoration Annotator (OCRA), the Virtual Transcription Laboratory (VTL), and the Collection Ingestion Tool (CIT).

docs.title: Sister Projects and Cascading Grant Applications
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_SISTER_PROJECTS_AND_CASCADING
  • Level: 2
  • Description: This group consists of specialized tools developed by sister projects as well as applications developed by institutions through the Cascading Grants Programme.

docs.title: Commercial Companies and Other Adopters
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_COMMERCIAL_COMPANIES_AND
  • Level: 2
  • Description: This category encompasses tools provided by the broader ecosystem, including private companies.

docs.title: Infrastructure and Digital Services
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_INFRASTRUCTURE_AND_DIGITAL
  • Level: 1
  • Description: The infrastructure that will provide access to CH data, metadata and digital services in a secure, federated environment. Sample services include user access management, data storage, data sharing, computing resources, search capabilities, workflow management, and Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) services.

docs.title: ECHOES Infrastructure and Services
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_ECHOES_INFRASTRUCTURE_AND
  • Level: 2
  • Description: ECHOES is developing the core infrastructure and cloud components. These include the Single Entry Point (SEP), and services such as the Authentication and Authorisation Infrastructure (AAI) and the core Knowledge Base.

docs.title: Sister Projects and Cascading Grant Services
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_SISTER_PROJECTS_AND_CASCADING_GRANT
  • Level: 2
  • Description: These providers contribute specialized digital services and domain-specific tools.

docs.title: RIs and National/International Services
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_RIS_AND_NATIONAL
  • Level: 2
  • Description: These providers can offer federated nodes, distributed storage, and advanced computing resources. This category includes established European Research Infrastructures like DARIAH, CLARIN, and E-RIHS, national initiatives (e.g., ESPADON, H2IOSC), and large-scale providers of High-Performance Computing (HPC) and AI Factories (e.g., EuroHPC).

docs.title: Community Services
docs.text:
• ID: COMPONENTS_BY_PROVIDER_COMMUNITY_SERVICES
  • Level: 1

docs.title: ECHOES Services
docs.text:

  • Level: 2
  • Description: Services provided by ECHOES team to ensure project-wide awareness and technical baseline readiness. Examples include training modules and consortium-led webinars [D5.3].

docs.text:

docs.title: Summary of evaluation basis, core dimensions and expected outputs
docs.text:
The following summary tables provide a compact overview of how the Cloud Evalution Framework is planned to be applied to the main resource categories considered in deliverable D9.1. For each category, the tables identify the main evaluation basis, the core dimensions or relevant criteria, the principal types of assessment instruments, the expected assessment output, and the evidence that resource providers or other relevant contributors should prepare. These tables also support navigation across the more detailed resource-specific subsections of D9.1 deliverable.

A note on prioritization of evaluation dimensions

Core dimensions are essential for the initial implementation phase. Context-dependent dimensions are relevant only to certain resource categories or use cases. Not currently prioritized dimensions, although relevant, are not part of the first implementation phase of the framework. The tables below currently focus on core dimensions.

docs.title: Summary for Metadata and Knowledge Graph Data
Summary for Infrastructure and Digital Services
Summary for ECCCH as a Cloud
AI-enabled Resources
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.9 — Evaluation of AI-enabled resources Evaluation basis
  • Description of the AI-enabled functionality
  • Intended use and use context
  • Risk-related documentation
  • Transparency and user-information materials
  • Performance, reliability or robustness evidence where available
  • Human oversight and accountability information
  • Pilot or user feedback where relevant
Core dimensions / criteria
  • Risk classification and regulatory relevance
  • Transparency and explainability
  • Human oversight and accountability
  • Performance reliability and robustness
Main instruments
  • AI risk self-assessment
  • Regulatory relevance screening
  • Documentation review
  • Transparency and explainability review
  • Performance, reliability and robustness assessment
  • Human oversight and accountability review
  • Pilot-based or user-based evaluation where relevant
Expected output
  • Evidence on AI-related risks and regulatory relevance
  • Assessment of transparency, explainability, oversight and accountability
  • Evidence on performance reliability and robustness where available
  • Identification of limitations, safeguards or improvement needs
  • Input for later monitoring and governance of AI-enabled resources
What resource providers should prepare
  • Description of the AI-enabled component or functionality
  • Intended users, use cases and operating context
  • Available risk classification or risk assessment evidence
  • Documentation explaining how the AI-enabled resource works at an appropriate level
  • Performance, reliability or robustness evidence where available
  • Information on human oversight, accountability and escalation procedures
  • Known limitations and safeguards

docs.title: Community Services
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.8 — Evaluation of Community Services Evaluation basis
  • Training, engagement, support or capacity-building activity records
  • Participant registrations and attendance evidence
  • Questionnaires, feedback forms or user-study outputs
  • Community engagement evidence
  • Follow-up or reuse evidence where available
Core dimensions / criteria
  • Reach
  • Participant experience
Main instruments
  • Registration and attendance records
  • Participant questionnaires
  • Training or event feedback forms
  • Engagement analytics where appropriate
  • Follow-up surveys or interviews where appropriate
  • Pilot or activity evaluation reports
Expected output
  • Evidence on reach and participant experience
  • Indicators on participation, stakeholder groups and engagement
  • Feedback on relevance, usefulness and perceived value
  • Recommendations for improving accessibility, delivery or community relevance
What resource providers should prepare
  • Description of the community service or activity
  • Target audience and objectives
  • Registration and attendance data
  • Feedback forms or questionnaire results
  • Materials, documentation or outputs from the activity
  • Evidence of follow-up, reuse or continued engagement where available

docs.title: Data usage
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.4 — Evaluation of data usage Evaluation basis
  • Access, reuse or interaction evidence related to data resources
  • Logs and analytics where available and appropriate
  • Download, access, query or integration records
  • Provider-supplied usage information where usage is not captured directly by ECCCH services
  • Pilot or user-study evidence concerning data reuse
Core dimensions / criteria
  • Data usage
Main instruments
  • Usage analytics
  • Access, download or query statistics
  • Provider-supplied usage reports
  • Pilot evaluation outputs
  • Reuse evidence from workflows, services or applications
Expected output
  • Evidence of access, uptake or reuse of data resources
  • Usage indicators suitable for later aggregation or interpretation
  • Identification of resources with limited, emerging or significant reuse
  • Evidence supporting monitoring of uptake and value over time
What resource providers should prepare
  • Description of how the data resource is accessed
  • Available usage, access or download statistics
  • Known examples of reuse
  • Information on external access channels not visible to ECCCH analytics
  • Relevant pilot or user feedback where available

docs.title: Digital Assets
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.3 — Evaluation of Digital Assets Evaluation basis
  • Digital asset files or access links
  • Technical metadata
  • Digitization, acquisition or processing documentation
  • Paradata where available
  • Use-case information relevant to assessing fitness for purpose
Core dimensions / criteria
  • No single uniform core dimension set is defined for all digital asset types in D9.1
  • Asset-type-specific quality criteria should be refined through later CAF implementation and the wiki
  • Data usage, where relevant to access, uptake and reuse
Main instruments
  • Asset-type-specific quality assessment methods
  • Technical metadata and paradata review
  • Expert review where automated assessment is insufficient
  • Reference-based or no-reference quality assessment approaches where appropriate
  • Use-case-specific assessment criteria
Expected output
  • Evidence on the quality and fitness for purpose of the digital asset
  • Identification of asset-specific quality issues affecting reuse or interpretation
  • Documentation of relevant technical, acquisition or processing context
  • Recommendations for further assessment or improvement where needed
What resource providers should prepare
  • Access to the digital asset or representative sample
  • Technical metadata
  • Digitization, acquisition or processing documentation
  • Information on intended use cases
  • Known quality limitations or uncertainty information
  • Existing quality-control reports where available

docs.title: ECCCH as a Cloud
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.10 — Evaluation of the ECCCH as a Cloud Evaluation basis
  • ECCCH strategic objectives
  • Cloud-level KPIs and indicators
  • Resource and service inventories
  • Usage, participation, training and engagement evidence
  • Evidence of openness, reuse, collaboration and outputs
  • Governance and sustainability evidence
  • Qualitative interpretation and contextual evidence
Core dimensions / criteria
  • Ecosystem availability and interoperability
  • Participation and ecosystem reach
  • Training, engagement, and capacity building
  • Openness and reusability
  • Meaningful reuse and adoption
  • Perceived usefulness and community value
  • Collaboration and co-creation
  • Scientific and professional contribution
  • Sustainability and institutional readiness
Main instruments
  • Strategic KPI reporting
  • Resource and service inventories
  • Usage and participation monitoring
  • Training and engagement reporting
  • Survey and pilot evaluation synthesis
  • Collaboration and output tracking
  • Governance and sustainability review
  • Narrative interpretation of quantitative indicators
Expected output
  • Cloud-level strategic monitoring evidence
  • Aggregated view of availability, participation, reuse, value, collaboration, outputs and sustainability
  • Evidence supporting governance, prioritization and continuous improvement
  • Contextual interpretation of quantitative indicators
  • Identification of gaps, risks or areas requiring further development
What resource providers should prepare
  • Updated information on resources and services contributed to the ECCCH
  • Evidence of accessibility, interoperability, openness or reuse where relevant
  • Usage, participation or engagement evidence where available
  • Examples of reuse, collaboration or outputs enabled by the resource
  • Relevant documentation, repository links or Knowledge Base references
  • Maintenance, governance or sustainability information where relevant

docs.title: Infrastructure and Digital Services
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.7 — Evaluation of Infrastructure and Digital Services Evaluation basis

docs.title: Metadata and Knowledge Graph Data
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.2 — Evaluation of Metadata and Knowledge Graph Data Evaluation basis
  • Metadata records
  • Knowledge graph data
  • Schemas, application profiles, validation rules or equivalent documentation
  • Identifiers, labels, properties, links, licences and rights information
  • Evidence produced through validation, profiling or review procedures
Core dimensions / criteria
  • Structural Completeness
  • Property Coverage
  • Multilingual Labeling Coverage
  • Foundational and Format Consistency
  • Accessibility and Connectivity
  • Licensing (Rights)
  • Conformance to Standards
Main instruments
  • Metadata and knowledge graph validation tools
  • Schema, profile or constraint-based checks
  • Completeness and coverage analysis
  • FAIRness and interoperability-oriented checks
  • Licence, rights and provenance review
  • Expert or curator review where semantic interpretation is required
Expected output
  • Structured evidence on metadata or knowledge graph quality
  • Assessment of completeness, consistency, accessibility, connectivity, licensing and standards conformance
  • Identification of gaps, inconsistencies or improvement needs
  • Evidence that can support quality review and later indicator computation
What resource providers should prepare
  • Access to the metadata or knowledge graph resource
  • Documentation of the data model, schema, ontology, application profile or mappings used
  • Information on identifiers, labels, links, licences, rights and provenance
  • Available validation reports or quality-control evidence
  • Known limitations or pending improvements

docs.title: Semantic Resources
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.5 — Evaluation of Semantic Resources Evaluation basis
  • Ontology, vocabulary, taxonomy, thesaurus or semantic mapping
  • Documentation and usage guidance
  • Scope, purpose and intended use
  • Mappings or alignments with other semantic resources
  • Evidence on understandability, interoperability and reuse
Core dimensions / criteria
  • Usability and Understandability
  • Interoperability and Compatibility
Main instruments

  • Consistency and compatibility checks
  • Mapping and alignment review

  • User or community feedback where appropriate
Expected output
  • Evidence on usability, understandability, interoperability and compatibility
  • Identification of documentation gaps, ambiguous concepts or alignment issues
  • Assessment evidence supporting review and improvement of the semantic resource
  • Recommendations for clarification, extension or better integration
What resource providers should prepare
  • Machine-readable semantic resource file or access point
  • Human-readable documentation
  • Statement of scope and intended use
  • Examples of use where available
  • Mappings or alignments with relevant standards, vocabularies or ontologies
  • Versioning or maintenance information

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
Primary section Section 6.6 — Evaluation of Software Evaluation basis
  • Software codebase or repository
  • Software documentation
  • Licence and reuse information
  • Installation, deployment or execution information

Core dimensions / criteria
  • Compatibility
  • Open-Source Software
  • FAIRness
  • Security
  • Usability
Main instruments
  • Repository and documentation review
  • Software quality checklists
  • Static analysis or code-quality tools where appropriate
  • Testing and release evidence review
  • Security-oriented checks
  • FAIR software assessment instruments
  • User feedback or pilot-based usability evaluation
Expected output
  • Software quality self-assessment evidence
  • Evidence on compatibility, openness, FAIRness, security and usability
  • Identification of documentation, maintainability, security or reuse issues
  • Evidence supporting ECHOES quality review and improvement planning
What resource providers should prepare
  • Repository URL or source-code package
  • Licence information
  • README, installation and usage documentation
  • Information on dependencies, releases and maintenance status
  • Available test, quality or security reports
  • Description of intended users and use context

docs.title: Assessment Tools
docs.text:
An assessment instrument, or simply an assessment tool, is a software application, service, or methodology that facilitates the collection of measurements used to compute specific metrics or indicators.

This section documents the assessment tools currently identified within the framework. Each tool collection is described through its own index page, and each individual tool is documented on a dedicated page.

  • Software quality tools — Software quality related tools.
  • Software functional tools — Functional testing or validation tools.
  • Software E2E testing tools — End-to-end testing tools.
  • Software static analysis tools — Static analysis tools.
  • Software performance tools — Performance analysis tools.
  • Software security tools — Security-related tools.
  • Ontology tools — Ontology-oriented assessment or support tools.
  • KG tools — Knowledge graph and data-oriented tools.

docs.title: Kg Tools
docs.text: Knowledge graph and data-oriented tools.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• SHACL Engines (Apache Jena SHACL) — Validate RDF data against SHACL shapes enforcing structural and logical constraints.
  • SHACL Engines (pySHACL) — Python-based SHACL validator for RDF graphs.
  • ShEx (Shape Expressions) Validators (ShEx.js) — Validates RDF data against Shape Expressions schemas, especially suited for entity-centric datasets.
  • Openllet Reasoner — OWL 2 DL reasoner for ontology consistency checking and inference.
  • RDFUnit — Rule-based validation framework using SPARQL test patterns to detect structural issues and missing links.
  • Schematron — Rule-based validation language used to enforce standard compliance and structural rules.
  • Metadata Quality Toolkit — Computes metrics for field completeness and textual readability in metadata records.
  • KGTK (Knowledge Graph Toolkit) — Toolkit for profiling, connectivity analysis, and exploration of large knowledge graphs.
  • Silk Link Discovery Framework — Discovers and validates links across datasets using similarity rules and can detect duplicates.
  • LODsyndesis — Indexes links across many knowledge graphs and computes sameAs closures to detect duplicates.
  • RDFind — Detects structural dependencies and patterns in RDF data to identify missing or expected properties.
  • F-UJI — Automatically evaluates FAIR indicators related to accessibility and interoperability.
  • FAIR-Checker — Validates FAIR compliance directly on RDF metadata using SPARQL and SHACL.
  • FAIR Evaluator — Executes machine-readable FAIR maturity indicator tests using web and API interrogation.
  • FAIR-Shake — Combines automated FAIR checks with human-evaluated rubrics.
  • TripleCheckMate — Crowdsourcing platform for human validation of RDF triples selected automatically.
  • KGEval — Uses statistical inference to minimize human effort in validating factual correctness of triples.
  • Manual Metadata Quality Checklists — Human-curated checklists used by institutions to assess qualitative metadata aspects.
  • SHACL Playground — A constraint validator for the Shapes Constraint Language, written in JavaScript.

docs.title: Ontology Tools
docs.text: Ontology-oriented assessment or support tools.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Protégé Desktop — Free, open-source OWL ontology editor with built-in integration to DL reasoners and support for debugging inconsistencies and refactoring ontologies.
  • WebProtégé — Web-based collaborative ontology development environment supporting OWL 2, permissions, discussions, and change tracking for team-based ontology maintenance.
  • OOPS! (OntOlogy Pitfall Scanner!) — Web-based ontology evaluation tool that detects common ontology “pitfalls” that can lead to modeling errors and quality issues.
  • HermiT OWL Reasoner — OWL 2 DL reasoner that checks ontology consistency and computes class hierarchies (classification) and related inferences.
  • ELK Reasoner — High-performance OWL 2 EL reasoner for large ontologies; used to classify and validate profile-consistent ontologies efficiently.
  • Openllet (Pellet-derived) Reasoner — OWL 2 reasoner (Pellet lineage) for checking ontology consistency, classification, and inference services.
  • ROBOT — Command-line tool (OBO/OWL-focused) for automating ontology workflows (e.g., validation steps, reports, transforms) commonly used in ontology engineering pipelines.
  • OntoMetrics — Web-based tool that validates and displays statistics/metrics about a given ontology (upload OWL/RDF and obtain metric summaries).
  • Ontospy — Python library/CLI for inspecting and visualizing RDF/OWL vocabularies, extracting ontology structure and generating lightweight documentation.
  • WIDOCO — Wizard to generate enriched, customizable ontology documentation (terms, sections, visualizations) for publishing and reuse.
  • Pletka — Pletka is a platform for collaboratively creating, documenting, and sharing semantic data models.

docs.title: Software E2E Testing Tools
docs.text: End-to-end testing tools.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Selenium WebDriver — Widely used open-source framework for automating web browsers and performing functional end-to-end tests using the W3C WebDriver standard.
  • Playwright — Modern cross-browser end-to-end testing framework maintained by Microsoft, designed for reliable automation of web applications.
  • Cypress — JavaScript-first end-to-end testing framework that runs inside the browser, aimed at modern single-page applications and rich frontends.
  • Puppeteer — Node.js library for controlling Chromium and other browsers via DevTools or WebDriver BiDi, often used for lightweight end-to-end tests and browser automation.
  • WebdriverIO — Open-source automation framework for Node.js that wraps WebDriver and WebDriver BiDi, supporting web, mobile and component testing.
  • TestCafe — End-to-end testing framework for web applications that does not require WebDriver or browser plugins and runs on Node.js.
  • Nightwatch.js — Integrated end-to-end testing framework for web applications based on the W3C WebDriver API, with built-in test runner.
  • Galen Framework — Framework focused on testing layout and responsive design in addition to functional behavior, built on top of Selenium.
  • Appium — Open-source automation framework for testing native, hybrid and mobile web applications on iOS, Android and other platforms using the WebDriver protocol.

docs.title: Selenium WebDriver
docs.text:
• ID: SOFTWARE_E_E_SELENIUM_WEBDRIVER
  • Dimensions covered: Functionality (E2E testing).
  • Description: Widely used open-source framework for automating web browsers and performing functional end-to-end tests using the W3C WebDriver standard.
  • Link: https://www.selenium.dev

docs.title: Additional properties
docs.text:
• Adoption Likelihood: High.
  • Type of tool: End-to-end framework.
  • Interactions: Mouse clicks, typing into inputs, selecting from menus, form submission, navigation across pages, scrolling, handling alerts and frames.
  • Supported languages (not exhaustive list): Java, Python, JavaScript, C#, Ruby and others.
  • Target: Web applications.
  • Advantages: It is the industry standard, so is extremly matured.
  • Open Source: It is open-source.
  • Limitations: Selenium IDE add-on not compatible with some browsers (e.g. Chrome); Selenium IDE add-on might generate volatile event IDs. Slower than modern tools.

docs.title: Playwright
docs.text:
• ID: SOFTWARE_E_E_PLAYWRIGHT
  • Dimensions covered: Functionality (E2E testing).
  • Description: Modern cross-browser end-to-end testing framework maintained by Microsoft, designed for reliable automation of web applications.
  • Link: https://playwright.dev

docs.title: Additional properties
docs.text:
• Adoption Likelihood: High.
  • Type of tool: End-to-end framework.
  • Interactions: Mouse clicks and double-clicks, hover, drag and drop, typing, file uploads, multi-tab and multi-page scenarios, mobile emulation.
  • Supported languages (not exhaustive list): TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Java, .NET.
  • Target: Web applications.
  • Advantages: Fast, reliable, modern API, It has Auto-waiting and contextual actions.
  • Open Source: It is open-source.
  • Limitations: It has a smaller community than others but rapidly growing, It is primarily JS/TS-centric.

docs.title: Cypress
docs.text:
• ID: SOFTWARE_E_E_CYPRESS
  • Dimensions covered: Functionality (E2E testing).
  • Description: JavaScript-first end-to-end testing framework that runs inside the browser, aimed at modern single-page applications and rich frontends.
  • Link: https://www.cypress.io

docs.title: Additional properties
docs.text:
• Adoption Likelihood: Low.
  • Type of tool: End-to-end framework.
  • Interactions: Clicks, typing, selecting options, navigation, network request stubbing, assertions on DOM state, screenshots and video recording.
  • Supported languages (not exhaustive list): JavaScript, TypeScript.
  • Target: Web applications.
  • Advantages: It has a very friendly developer experience, It has Auto-waiting, Tests run in real browser context.
  • Open Source: It has a free/open-source core but a paid Dashboard for advanced test insights.
  • Limitations: Doesn't support multiple real tabs/windows well.

docs.title: Software Functional Tools
docs.text: Functional testing or validation tools.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• ISO/IEC 25040 – Evaluation Process (SQuaRE) — Defines a standard process model for planning, executing and concluding software quality evaluations, ensuring that assessments are systematic, repeatable and auditable.
  • Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Provides a structured artefact to map functional requirements to implementation and test artefacts, supporting monitoring of functional completeness and correctness.
  • Use-case / workflow coverage checklist — Provides a checklist-based approach to verify whether end-to-end cultural heritage workflows are fully supported by available system functions.
  • Automated functional test suites + coverage tools (e.g. JUnit/pytest/Jest with JaCoCo, coverage.py, Istanbul) — Provides automated execution and measurement of functional tests to verify correctness of implemented behaviour and quantify test coverage of functional requirements.
  • OpenAPI-based API contract testing (e.g. Dredd, Schemathesis) — Provides automated validation of API behaviour against formally specified OpenAPI contracts to ensure functional correctness and robustness at the interface level.
  • Property-based / fuzz testing libraries (e.g. Hypothesis; Schemathesis property-based mode) — Provides automated generation and execution of test cases based on properties and invariants to detect functional defects under a wide range of input conditions.
  • ISO 25010-based Functional Suitability Questionnaire — Provides a structured questionnaire to capture end-user perceptions of functional completeness, correctness and appropriateness of software services.
  • Structured interview protocol with cultural heritage professionals — Provides a qualitative interview-based method to collect in-depth feedback from domain professionals on functional gaps, correctness issues and workflow fit.
  • Task-based user test protocol (scenario-based functional evaluation) — Provides a scenario-based testing protocol to evaluate whether users can successfully complete representative tasks using available system functions.
  • Usage analytics dashboards for ECCCH services — Provides analytics-based insight into real-world usage of system functions to assess functional appropriateness, detect underused features and identify functional gaps.
  • Data validation and semantic consistency test suites (e.g. SHACL, SPARQL-based checks) — Provides automated validation of data and semantic outputs against defined constraints to ensure functional correctness and consistency at the data level.
  • Workflow-oriented functional coverage matrix for Digital Twins — Provides a matrix-based artefact to assess whether required Digital Twin operations are supported across tools and services within defined workflows.
  • Domain expert review checklist (heritage-specific functional appropriateness) — Provides a domain-specific expert checklist to assess whether system functions appropriately support cultural heritage research and professional practices.

docs.title: Software Performance Tools
docs.text: Performance analysis tools.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Google Lighthouse — Automated auditing tool for web pages that reports performance, accessibility, best practices and SEO scores based on lab tests in a real or headless browser.
  • WebPageTest (self-hosted) — Deep web performance testing and diagnostics tool that runs scripted browser sessions and produces detailed waterfalls, visual metrics and Core Web Vitals.
  • PageSpeed Insights — Free Google service that combines Lighthouse lab tests with field data from the Chrome User Experience Report to rate page performance on mobile and desktop.
  • Sitespeed.io — Open-source toolkit that orchestrates browsers and tools such as Browsertime and Lighthouse to collect and store web performance metrics over time.
  • Apache JMeter — Open-source Java application for load, stress and functional testing of web applications and a wide range of networked services.
  • Grafana k6 (k6 OSS) — Modern open-source load testing tool and engine where test scripts are written in JavaScript and run via a CLI for HTTP and other protocols.
  • Grafana — Open-source analytics and visualization platform that builds dashboards and alerts on top of metrics, logs and traces from various data sources.
  • Gatling (Community Edition) — Open-source load and performance testing tool for web applications and APIs, based on asynchronous non-blocking architecture.
  • Locust — Open-source load testing framework where user behavior is defined in Python code and executed by a scalable swarm of workers.
  • Prometheus — Open-source systems monitoring and time-series database used to collect, store and query metrics from applications and infrastructure.

docs.title: Google Lighthouse
docs.text:
• ID: SOFTWARE_PERFORMANCE_TOOLS_GOOGLE_LIGHTHOUSE
  • Dimensions covered: performance (load times, Core Web Vitals), accessibility, best practices, SEO.
  • Description: Automated auditing tool for web pages that reports performance, accessibility, best practices and SEO scores based on lab tests in a real or headless browser.
  • Link: https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse

docs.title: Additional properties
docs.text:
• Category: web performance audit.
  • Target layer: web frontend.
  • Target environment: web application.
  • Resource type measured: browser loading behavior, DOM rendering, JavaScript execution.
  • Open source: yes.
  • Delivery model: CLI tool, browser DevTools integration, Node.js library, CI integrations.
  • Self-hosted possible: yes.
  • Scripting / configuration language: JavaScript / JSON configuration.

docs.title: WebPageTest (self-hosted)
docs.text:
• ID: SOFTWARE_PERFORMANCE_TOOLS_WEBPAGETEST_SELF_HOSTED
  • Dimensions covered: performance (page load, rendering, Core Web Vitals), network behavior, caching efficiency.
  • Description: Deep web performance testing and diagnostics tool that runs scripted browser sessions and produces detailed waterfalls, visual metrics and Core Web Vitals.
  • Link: https://github.com/catchpoint/WebPageTest

docs.title: Software Quality Tools
docs.text: Software quality related tools.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• ISO/IEC 25010 software product quality model — Defines a standardised model of software product quality characteristics and sub-characteristics, providing a common vocabulary and reference framework for specifying, evaluating and comparing software quality.
  • ISO/IEC 25023 measurement of system and software product quality — Provides a standard catalogue of quantitative measures for assessing system and software product quality characteristics defined in ISO/IEC 25010.
  • ISO/IEC 5055 Automated Source Code Quality Measures — Defines automated measures for assessing the structural quality of source code, focusing on maintainability, reliability, performance efficiency and security weaknesses.
  • OMG ASCQM (Automated Source Code Quality Measures) v1.1 — OMG specification for automatically measuring software structural quality through static source-code analysis. It defines a catalogue of weaknesses and detection patterns; scores per quality dimension are computed by counting and aggregating occurrences of these weaknesses by quality characteristic. The specification also defines conformance requirements to ensure that measurements are automated, objective, transparent, and repeatable.
  • Language-specific linters and formatters (e.g. ESLint, Pylint, Flake8, RuboCop, golangci-lint) — Automatically analyse source code to enforce coding standards, detect common defects and improve consistency and basic correctness across different programming languages.
  • Radon (Python code metrics) — Computes code complexity, maintainability and size metrics for Python codebases to support early identification of hard-to-maintain modules.
  • Visual Studio (IDE) Code Metrics (Maintainability Index and related metrics) — Provides IDE-integrated metrics for analysing maintainability, complexity and structural properties of object-oriented code, mainly for .NET projects.
  • Chidamber Kemerer (CK) metrics suite (e.g. via CKJM or similar tools) — Offers a widely used suite of object-oriented design metrics to assess complexity, coupling, cohesion and inheritance-related maintainability risks.
  • DORA Four Key Metrics (Four Keys) — Defines a set of four key metrics to measure software delivery performance and operational reliability in DevOps and continuous delivery environments.
  • Defect and incident metrics from issue trackers (e.g. GitHub/GitLab Issues, Jira) — Derives defect, incident and resolution metrics from issue and incident tracking systems to support analysis of software reliability and quality trends.
  • Test coverage tools (e.g. JaCoCo, Istanbul/nyc, pytest-cov) — Measure the proportion of source code exercised by automated tests, providing indirect indicators of defect risk and test adequacy.
  • DORA “Documentation quality” capability — Provides a capability-based assessment framework to evaluate the quality of internal documentation using structured, survey-based indicators.
  • Documentation quality metrics and checklists (e.g. “Evaluating Software Documentation Quality” tooling) — Offer research-based metrics and checklists to systematically assess the completeness, structure and usability of software documentation.
  • OpenSSF Best Practices Badge — Provides a checklist-based assessment and public badge to evaluate and communicate adherence to recognised best practices in open-source software projects.
  • FAIR for Research Software (FAIR4RS) Principles — Defines principles and assessment criteria to improve the findability, accessibility, interoperability and reusability of research software.
  • SPDX specification — SPDX is a standardized format for describing the components of a software system. It provides a common way for development teams to document what is included in their software, typically in the form of a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM). In addition to listing software components, SPDX can also capture information about licenses, security aspects, data, and AI related elements. The main purpose of SPDX is to improve transparency and risk assessment by making it easier to understand which third party components are used, whether known security vulnerabilities exist, which licenses apply, and what additional elements, such as data or AI models, are involved. This facilitates review by customers, auditors, and security teams, and supports compliance, security analysis, and supply chain management. Many tools support the SPDX specification: https://spdx.dev/use/spdx-tools/.

docs.title: Software Security Tools
docs.text: Security-related tools.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• OWASP Application Security Verification Standard (ASVS) — A standard that defines security requirements and verification levels for designing, building, and testing secure web applications and APIs.
  • OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (WSTG) — A comprehensive guide that provides methodologies and test cases for performing web application security testing and penetration testing.
  • OWASP Zed Attack Proxy (ZAP) — An open-source dynamic application security testing tool for finding vulnerabilities in running web applications through automated and manual testing.
  • Bandit (Python security linter) — A static analysis tool that identifies common security issues and insecure coding practices in Python source code.
  • OWASP Software Assurance Maturity Model (SAMM) — A framework for measuring and improving the maturity of secure software development practices across governance, design, implementation, and operations.
  • CIS Critical Security Controls — A prioritized set of best-practice security controls to improve an organisation’s overall cybersecurity posture and resilience.
  • CIS Benchmarks — Configuration best practices and hardening guidelines for operating systems, cloud services, containers, and applications.
  • Cloud Security Alliance Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) and CAIQ — A cloud security control framework and questionnaire that helps assess and communicate cloud security capabilities and assurance.
  • ENISA Cloud Computing Security Risk Assessment and Information Assurance Framework — Guidance and frameworks from ENISA for assessing, managing, and assuring cloud computing security risks.
  • OWASP Dependency-Check — A tool that identifies known vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies by scanning project dependency manifests.
  • OSV-Scanner — A command-line tool that scans source code, dependencies, and SBOMs for known vulnerabilities using the OSV database.
  • Trivy — A security scanner for containers, cloud-native workloads, dependencies, and infrastructure-as-code configurations.
  • SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) — A framework that defines maturity levels and best practices for securing software supply chains and build processes.
  • CHAOSS Libyears and related risk metrics — A set of metrics that quantify how outdated software dependencies are and help assess open-source dependency risk.
  • OpenSSF Scorecard — An automated tool that assesses open-source projects against a set of security best practices and produces a security score.
  • OWASP DefectDojo — An open-source vulnerability management platform that aggregates, tracks, and prioritises security findings from multiple tools.
  • Bearer CLI — Bearer CLI is a static application security testing (SAST) tool that scans your source code and analyzes your data flows to discover, filter and prioritize security and privacy risks.

docs.title: KG tools
docs.text: Knowledge graph and data-oriented tools.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Shapes Constraint Language (SHACL) — A W3C language for validating RDF graphs against a set of conditions. These conditions are provided as shapes and other constructs expressed in the form of an RDF graph.
  • Apache Jena SHACL — Apache implementation of the W3C SHACL. It implements SHACL Core and SHACL SPARQL Constraints.
  • pySHACL — Python-based SHACL validator for RDF graphs.
  • SHACL Playground — A constraint validator for the Shapes Constraint Language, written in JavaScript.
  • SHACL Play! — Free suite of tools from Sparna to work with SHACL.
  • Shape Expressions — A structural schema language for validation, traversal and transformation of RDF graphs.
  • ShEx.js — Shape Expressions validator for JavaScript.
  • RDFUnit — Rule-based validation framework using SPARQL test patterns to detect structural issues and missing links.
  • F-UJI — A web service to programatically assess FAIRness of research data objects based on metrics developed by the FAIRsFAIR project.
  • FAIR-Checker — A web and command line tool to assess FAIRness of web resources.
  • FAIR Evaluator — Resources and guidelines to assess the FAIRness of digital resources.
  • FAIR-Shake — Combines automated FAIR checks with human-evaluated rubrics.
  • TripleCheckMate — Crowdsourcing platform for human validation of RDF triples selected automatically.
  • CrowdHeritage — Open crowdsourcing platform for cultural heritage institutions to run campaigns in which contributors enrich and validate digitised cultural heritage metadata by adding annotations or reviewing existing ones.
  • Europeana methodology for validating enrichments — Methodology used by the Europeana Foundation to validate metadata enrichment efforts before acceptance. It combines documentation of the enrichment context, representative sampling, manual or semi-automated assessment of sample outputs, validation against agreed quality requirements, and, where relevant, calibration of confidence thresholds and iterative improvement of the enrichment process.

docs.title: Ontology tools
docs.text: Ontology-oriented assessment or support tools.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Protégé Desktop — Free, open-source OWL ontology editor with built-in integration to DL reasoners and support for debugging inconsistencies and refactoring ontologies.
  • WebProtégé — Web-based collaborative ontology development environment supporting OWL 2, permissions, discussions, and change tracking for team-based ontology maintenance.
  • OOPS! (OntOlogy Pitfall Scanner!) — Web-based ontology evaluation tool that detects common ontology “pitfalls” that can lead to modeling errors and quality issues.
  • HermiT OWL Reasoner — OWL 2 DL reasoner that checks ontology consistency and computes class hierarchies (classification) and related inferences.
  • ELK Reasoner — High-performance OWL 2 EL reasoner for large ontologies; used to classify and validate profile-consistent ontologies efficiently.
  • Openllet (Pellet-derived) Reasoner — OWL 2 reasoner (Pellet lineage) for checking ontology consistency, classification, and inference services.
  • OntoMetrics — Web-based tool that validates and displays statistics/metrics about a given ontology (upload OWL/RDF and obtain metric summaries).
  • Ontospy — Python library/CLI for inspecting and visualizing RDF/OWL vocabularies, extracting ontology structure and generating lightweight documentation.
  • WIDOCO — Wizard to generate enriched, customizable ontology documentation (terms, sections, visualizations) for publishing and reuse.
  • Pletka — Pletka is a platform for collaboratively creating, documenting, and sharing semantic data models.
  • Framework for Ontology Quality Assessment (FOCA) — Methodology for ontology evaluation based on a Goal–Question–Metric approach. It includes a questionnaire and a scoring model to assess ontology quality beyond formal consistency, with questions about completeness, reuse, documentation, organizational fitness, and alignment.
  • Ontology Usability Scale (OUS) — A questionnaire-style usability instrument proposed specifically for ontologies, with short statements intended to assess ontology usability in terms of effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction, contextual relevance, maturity, intended use, and fitness for use. It is especially suitable for expert/user evaluation workshops or pilot-based assessment.
  • QASAR — Recent ontology quality-assurance platform that integrates multiple QA methodologies, interactive reports, and guided recommendations to support broader ontology evaluation.

docs.title: Software E2E testing tools
docs.text: End-to-end testing tools.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Selenium WebDriver — Widely used open-source framework for automating web browsers and performing functional end-to-end tests using the W3C WebDriver standard.
  • Playwright — Modern cross-browser end-to-end testing framework maintained by Microsoft, designed for reliable automation of web applications.
  • Puppeteer — Node.js library for controlling Chromium and other browsers via DevTools or WebDriver BiDi, often used for lightweight end-to-end tests and browser automation.
  • WebdriverIO — Open-source automation framework for Node.js that wraps WebDriver and WebDriver BiDi, supporting web, mobile and component testing.
  • TestCafe — End-to-end testing framework for web applications that does not require WebDriver or browser plugins and runs on Node.js.
  • Nightwatch.js — Integrated end-to-end testing framework for web applications based on the W3C WebDriver API, with built-in test runner.
  • Appium — Open-source automation framework for testing native, hybrid and mobile web applications on iOS, Android and other platforms using the WebDriver protocol.

docs.title: Software functional tools
docs.text: Functional testing or validation tools.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• ISO/IEC 25040 – Evaluation Process (SQuaRE) — Defines a standard process model for planning, executing and concluding software quality evaluations, ensuring that assessments are systematic, repeatable and auditable.
  • Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Provides a structured artefact to map functional requirements to implementation and test artefacts, supporting monitoring of functional completeness and correctness.
  • Automated functional test suites + coverage tools (e.g. JUnit/pytest/Jest with JaCoCo, coverage.py, Istanbul) — Provides automated execution and measurement of functional tests to verify correctness of implemented behaviour and quantify test coverage of functional requirements.
  • OpenAPI-based API contract testing (e.g. Dredd, Schemathesis) — Provides automated validation of API behaviour against formally specified OpenAPI contracts to ensure functional correctness and robustness at the interface level.
  • Property-based / fuzz testing libraries (e.g. Hypothesis; Schemathesis property-based mode) — Provides automated generation and execution of test cases based on properties and invariants to detect functional defects under a wide range of input conditions.
  • Task-based user test protocol — Provides a scenario-based testing protocol to evaluate whether users can successfully complete representative tasks using available system functions.

docs.title: Software performance tools
docs.text: Performance analysis tools.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Google Lighthouse — Automated auditing tool for web pages that reports performance, accessibility, best practices and SEO scores based on lab tests in a real or headless browser.
  • WebPageTest (self-hosted) — Deep web performance testing and diagnostics tool that runs scripted browser sessions and produces detailed waterfalls, visual metrics and Core Web Vitals.
  • PageSpeed Insights — Free Google service that combines Lighthouse lab tests with field data from the Chrome User Experience Report to rate page performance on mobile and desktop.
  • Sitespeed.io — Open-source toolkit that orchestrates browsers and tools such as Browsertime and Lighthouse to collect and store web performance metrics over time.
  • Apache JMeter — Open-source Java application for load, stress and functional testing of web applications and a wide range of networked services.

docs.title: Software quality tools
docs.text: Software quality related tools.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• ISO/IEC 25010 software product quality model — Defines a standardised model of software product quality characteristics and sub-characteristics, providing a common vocabulary and reference framework for specifying, evaluating and comparing software quality.
  • ISO/IEC 25023 measurement of system and software product quality — Provides a standard catalog of quantitative measures for assessing system and software product quality characteristics defined in ISO/IEC 25010.
  • ISO/IEC 5055 Automated Source Code Quality Measures — Defines automated measures for assessing the structural quality of source code, focusing on maintainability, reliability, performance efficiency and security weaknesses.
  • OMG ASCQM (Automated Source Code Quality Measures) v1.1 — OMG specification for automatically measuring software structural quality through static source-code analysis. It defines a catalog of weaknesses and detection patterns; scores per quality dimension are computed by counting and aggregating occurrences of these weaknesses by quality characteristic. The specification also defines conformance requirements to ensure that measurements are automated, objective, transparent, and repeatable.
  • Radon (Python code metrics) — Computes code complexity, maintainability and size metrics for Python codebases to support early identification of hard-to-maintain modules.
  • Visual Studio (IDE) Code Metrics (Maintainability Index and related metrics) — Provides IDE-integrated metrics for analysing maintainability, complexity and structural properties of object-oriented code, mainly for .NET projects.
  • Chidamber Kemerer (CK) metrics suite (e.g. via CKJM or similar tools) — Offers a widely used suite of object-oriented design metrics to assess complexity, coupling, cohesion and inheritance-related maintainability risks.
  • DORA Four Key Metrics (Four Keys) — Defines a set of four key metrics to measure software delivery performance and operational reliability in DevOps and continuous delivery environments.
  • DORA “Documentation quality” capability — Provides a capability-based assessment framework to evaluate the quality of internal documentation using structured, survey-based indicators.
  • OpenSSF Best Practices Badge — Provides a checklist-based assessment and public badge to evaluate and communicate adherence to recognised best practices in open-source software projects.
  • FAIR for Research Software (FAIR4RS) Principles — Defines principles and assessment criteria to improve the findability, accessibility, interoperability and reusability of research software.
  • SPDX specification — SPDX is a standardized format for describing the components of a software system. It provides a common way for development teams to document what is included in their software, typically in the form of a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM).

docs.title: Software security tools
docs.text: Security-related tools.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• OWASP Application Security Verification Standard (ASVS) — A standard that defines security requirements and verification levels for designing, building, and testing secure web applications and APIs.
  • OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (WSTG) — A comprehensive guide that provides methodologies and test cases for performing web application security testing and penetration testing.
  • OWASP Zed Attack Proxy (ZAP) — An open-source dynamic application security testing tool for finding vulnerabilities in running web applications through automated and manual testing.
  • Bandit (Python security linter) — A static analysis tool that identifies common security issues and insecure coding practices in Python source code.
  • OWASP Software Assurance Maturity Model (SAMM) — A framework for measuring and improving the maturity of secure software development practices across governance, design, implementation, and operations.
  • CIS Critical Security Controls — A prioritized set of best-practice security controls to improve an organisation’s overall cybersecurity posture and resilience.
  • CIS Benchmarks — Configuration best practices and hardening guidelines for operating systems, cloud services, containers, and applications.
  • Cloud Security Alliance Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) and CAIQ — A cloud security control framework and questionnaire that helps assess and communicate cloud security capabilities and assurance.
  • ENISA Cloud Computing Security Risk Assessment and Information Assurance Framework — Guidance and frameworks from ENISA for assessing, managing, and assuring cloud computing security risks.
  • OWASP Dependency-Check — A tool that identifies known vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies by scanning project dependency manifests.
  • OSV-Scanner — A command-line tool that scans source code, dependencies, and SBOMs for known vulnerabilities using the OSV database.
  • Trivy — A security scanner for containers, cloud-native workloads, dependencies, and infrastructure-as-code configurations.
  • SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) — A framework that defines maturity levels and best practices for securing software supply chains and build processes.
  • CHAOSS Libyears and related risk metrics — A set of metrics that quantify how outdated software dependencies are and help assess open-source dependency risk.
  • OpenSSF Scorecard — An automated tool that assesses open-source projects against a set of security best practices and produces a security score.
  • OWASP DefectDojo — An open-source vulnerability management platform that aggregates, tracks, and prioritises security findings from multiple tools.
  • Bearer CLI — Bearer CLI is a static application security testing (SAST) tool that scans your source code and analyzes your data flows to discover, filter and prioritize security and privacy risks.

  • PMD — Static analyzer for Java and other languages (includes JS support).
  • cppcheck — Static analysis tool for C and C++ detecting bugs and undefined behavior.
  • Clang-Tidy — Part of LLVM/Clang project; performs linting and static analysis for C/C++/Objective-C.
  • Infer — Static analysis tool from Meta for C, C++, Java, and Objective-C focused on bug detection.
  • OCLint — Analyzes C, C++, and Objective-C code for potential issues and style violations.
